final_answer:
The vulnerability is a validator-set-churn **DoS/liveness bug** in the `>2/3` admission vote, not scope creep.

Why this matters: with current logic, snapshot power stays in the denominator but a kicked validator can become ineligible to vote; an attacker can remove enough snapshot voters mid-proposal and make finalization unreachable, freezing validator onboarding or selectively blocking candidates.

What should be done next:
1. For an active nonce, validate voter eligibility from the nonce snapshot only.
2. Use live validator-set checks only when starting a new nonce.
3. Add the 3 regression tests already listed and rerun `go test ./x/stake/... -count=1`.

No, it is not fully production-ready yet; one real blocker remains in the >2/3 validator-vote flow.

Why this matters: voting power is snapshotted per proposal, but voter eligibility is still checked against the live current validator set on each vote. If a validator included in the snapshot leaves/is removed before voting, their power stays in the snapshot denominator while their vote becomes ineligible, which can leave approvals stuck pending.

What should be done next:
1. For pending proposals, validate voter eligibility from the proposal snapshot (snapshot voter-power entry), not from the live current set.
2. Keep live current-set validation only when opening a new proposal nonce.
3. Add regression tests for:
   1. snapshot voter no longer in current set can still vote,
   2. non-snapshot validator cannot vote,
   3. proposal still finalizes correctly under validator-set drift.
4. Re-run go test ./x/stake/... -count=1 after that patch.

Confirmed facts from this read-only audit: I re-read AGENTS.md, ran go test ./x/stake/keeper -v, and all current tests pass, but this snapshot-vs-live eligibility mismatch is still a functional gap.
